title: Mountain

infobox.value: is an elevated portion of the surface of a planet, generally with steep sides that show significant exposed [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock"). Although definitions vary, a mountain is usually considered higher than a [hill](/wiki/Hill "Hill"), typically rising 610 [metres](/wiki/Metre "Metre") (2,000 [ft](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) or more above the surrounding land. It may either have a limited [summit](/wiki/Summit "Summit") area or be a smaller [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") with high elevation and steep sides. A few mountains are [isolated summits](/wiki/Inselberg "Inselberg"), but most occur in [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range").[[2]](#cite_note-agi-1997-2)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Large natural elevation of the Earth's surface

For other uses, see [Mountain (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mountain_(disambiguation) "Mountain (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Everest_North_Face_toward_Base_Camp_Tibet_Luca_Galuzzi_2006.jpg/330px-Everest_North_Face_toward_Base_Camp_Tibet_Luca_Galuzzi_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Everest_North_Face_toward_Base_Camp_Tibet_Luca_Galuzzi_2006.jpg)

[Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest"), Earth's highest mountain

A **mountain** is an elevated portion of the surface of a planet, generally with steep sides that show significant exposed [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock"). Although definitions vary, a mountain is usually considered higher than a [hill](/wiki/Hill "Hill"), typically rising 610 [metres](/wiki/Metre "Metre") (2,000 [ft](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) or more above the surrounding land. It may either have a limited [summit](/wiki/Summit "Summit") area or be a smaller [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") with high elevation and steep sides. A few mountains are [isolated summits](/wiki/Inselberg "Inselberg"), but most occur in [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range").[[2]](#cite_note-agi-1997-2)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[Mountains are formed](/wiki/Mountain_formation "Mountain formation") through [tectonic forces](/wiki/Tectonic_plate "Tectonic plate"), [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), or [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism"),[[2]](#cite_note-agi-1997-2)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] which act on time scales of up to tens of millions of years.[[3]](#cite_note-levin-2010-83-3) Once mountain building ceases, mountains are slowly leveled through the action of [weathering](/wiki/Weathering "Weathering"), through [slumping](/wiki/Slump_(geology) "Slump (geology)") and other forms of [mass wasting](/wiki/Mass_wasting "Mass wasting"), as well as through erosion by [rivers](/wiki/River "River") and [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

High elevations on mountains produce [colder climates](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") than at [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") at similar latitude. These colder climates strongly affect the [ecosystems](/wiki/Montane_ecosystems "Montane ecosystems") of mountains: different elevations have different plants and animals. Because of the less hospitable terrain and climate, mountains tend to be used less for agriculture and more for resource extraction, such as [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") and [logging](/wiki/Logging "Logging"), along with recreation, such as [mountain climbing](/wiki/Mountain_climbing "Mountain climbing") and [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing").

The highest mountain on Earth is [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest") in the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), whose summit is 8,850 m (29,035 ft) above mean sea level. The [highest known mountain](/wiki/List_of_tallest_mountains_in_the_Solar_System "List of tallest mountains in the Solar System") on any planet in the Solar System is [Olympus Mons](/wiki/Olympus_Mons "Olympus Mons") on Mars at 21,171 m (69,459 ft). The highest mountain on Earth from base to peak is [Mauna Kea](/wiki/Mauna_Kea "Mauna Kea") in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), which rises 9,330 m (30,610 ft) from its base at the bottom of the ocean; some scientists consider it to be the tallest on Earth.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

## Definition

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Volc%C3%A1n_Chimborazo%2C_%22El_Taita_Chimborazo%22_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Volc%C3%A1n_Chimborazo%2C_%22El_Taita_Chimborazo%22_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volc%C3%A1n_Chimborazo,_%22El_Taita_Chimborazo%22_(cropped).jpg)

[Chimborazo](/wiki/Chimborazo "Chimborazo"), Ecuador, whose summit is the point farthest away from the Earth's center[[5]](#cite_note-Krulwich2007-5)

There is no universally accepted definition of a mountain. Elevation, volume, relief, steepness, spacing and continuity have been used as criteria for defining a mountain.[[6]](#cite_note-Gerrard1990-6) In the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* a mountain is defined as "a natural elevation of the earth surface rising more or less abruptly from the surrounding level and attaining an altitude which, relatively to the adjacent elevation, is impressive or notable."[[6]](#cite_note-Gerrard1990-6)

Whether a landform is called a mountain may depend on local usage. John Whittow's *Dictionary of Physical Geography*[[7]](#cite_note-whittow-7) states "Some authorities regard eminences above 600 metres (1,969 ft) as mountains, those below being referred to as hills."[[8]](#cite_note-8) Conversely, some lower mountain ranges with an average elevation between 1,500 metres (4,921 ft) and 2,000 metres (6,562 ft) may instead be called hills, such as the [Sivalik Hills](/wiki/Sivalik_Hills "Sivalik Hills") in the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas")[[9]](#cite_note-9) and the [Black Hills](/wiki/Black_Hills "Black Hills") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland, a mountain is usually defined as any summit at least 2,000 feet (610 m) high,[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) which accords with the official UK government's definition that a mountain, for the purposes of access, is a summit of 2,000 feet (610 m) or higher.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In addition, some definitions also include a [topographical prominence](/wiki/Topographical_prominence "Topographical prominence") requirement, such as that the mountain rises 300 metres (984 ft) above the surrounding terrain.[[2]](#cite_note-agi-1997-2) At one time, the [United States Board on Geographic Names](/wiki/United_States_Board_on_Geographic_Names "United States Board on Geographic Names") defined a mountain as being 1,000 feet (305 m) or taller,[[17]](#cite_note-17) but has abandoned the definition since the 1970s. Any similar landform lower than this height was considered a hill. However, today, the [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") concludes that these terms do not have technical definitions in the US.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Mountain_regions_identified_by_UNEP.png/330px-Mountain_regions_identified_by_UNEP.png)](/wiki/File:Mountain_regions_identified_by_UNEP.png)

Distribution of mountains by location and elevation

The [UN Environmental Programme](/wiki/UN_Environmental_Programme "UN Environmental Programme")'s definition of "mountainous environment" includes any of the following:[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19): 74

* Class 1: Elevation greater than 4,500 m (14,764 ft).
* Class 2: Elevation between 3,500 and 4,500 m (11,483 and 14,764 ft).
* Class 3: Elevation between 2,500 and 3,500 m (8,202 and 11,483 ft).
* Class 4: Elevation between 1,500 and 2,500 m (4,921 and 8,202 ft), with a slope greater than 2 degrees.
* Class 5: Elevation between 1,000 and 1,500 m (3,281 and 4,921 ft), with a slope greater than 5 degrees or 300 m (984 ft) elevation range within 7 km (4.3 mi).
* Class 6: Elevation between 300 and 1,000 m (984 and 3,281 ft), with a 300 m (984 ft) elevation range within 7 km (4.3 mi).
* Class 7: Isolated inner basins and plateaus less than 25 km2 (9.7 mi2) in area that are completely surrounded by Class 1 to 6 mountains, but do not themselves meet criteria for Class 1 to 6 mountains.

Using these definitions, mountains cover 33% of Eurasia, 19% of South America, 24% of North America, and 14% of Africa.[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19): 14 As a whole, 24% of the Earth's land mass is mountainous.[[20]](#cite_note-panos-20)

## Geology

Main articles: [Mountain formation](/wiki/Mountain_formation "Mountain formation") and [List of mountain types](/wiki/List_of_mountain_types "List of mountain types")

There are three main types of mountains: [volcanic](/wiki/Volcanic "Volcanic"), [fold](/wiki/Fold_mountain "Fold mountain"), and [block](/wiki/Fault-block_mountain "Fault-block mountain").[[21]](#cite_note-literacy-21) All three types are formed from [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics"): when portions of the Earth's crust move, crumple, and dive. Compressional forces, [isostatic](/wiki/Isostasy "Isostasy") [uplift](/wiki/Orogeny "Orogeny") and intrusion of [igneous matter](/wiki/Igneous_rock "Igneous rock") forces surface rock upward, creating a landform higher than the surrounding features. The height of the feature makes it either a hill or, if higher and steeper, a mountain. Major mountains tend to occur in long linear arcs, indicating tectonic plate boundaries and activity.

### Volcanoes

Main article: [Volcano](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/080103_hakkai_fuji.jpg/250px-080103_hakkai_fuji.jpg)](/wiki/File:080103_hakkai_fuji.jpg)

[Fuji volcano](/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji")

Volcanoes are formed when [a plate is pushed below another plate](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction"), or at a [mid-ocean ridge](/wiki/Mid-ocean_ridge "Mid-ocean ridge") or [hotspot](/wiki/Hotspot_(geology) "Hotspot (geology)").[[22]](#cite_note-Butz-22) At a depth of around 100 km (60 mi), melting occurs in rock above the slab (due to the addition of water), and forms [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma") that reaches the surface. When the magma reaches the surface, it often builds a volcanic mountain, such as a [shield volcano](/wiki/Shield_volcano "Shield volcano") or a [stratovolcano](/wiki/Stratovolcano "Stratovolcano").[[6]](#cite_note-Gerrard1990-6): 194 Examples of volcanoes include [Mount Fuji](/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji") in Japan and [Mount Pinatubo](/wiki/Mount_Pinatubo "Mount Pinatubo") in the Philippines. The magma does not have to reach the surface in order to create a mountain: magma that solidifies below ground can still form [dome mountains](/wiki/Dome_mountain "Dome mountain"), such as [Navajo Mountain](/wiki/Navajo_Mountain "Navajo Mountain") in the United States.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Fold mountains

Main article: [Fold mountains](/wiki/Fold_mountains "Fold mountains")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Lewis_overthrust_fault_nh10f.jpg/250px-Lewis_overthrust_fault_nh10f.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lewis_overthrust_fault_nh10f.jpg)

Illustration of mountains that developed on a [fold](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)") that has been [thrust](/wiki/Thrust_fault "Thrust fault")

Fold mountains occur when two plates collide: shortening occurs along thrust faults and the crust is overthickened.[[24]](#cite_note-Hatcher-24) Since the less dense [continental crust](/wiki/Continental_crust "Continental crust") "floats" on the denser [mantle](/wiki/Mantle_(geology) "Mantle (geology)") rocks beneath, the weight of any crustal material forced upward to form hills, [plateaus](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") or mountains must be [balanced by the buoyancy force](/wiki/Isostasy "Isostasy") of a much greater volume forced downward into the mantle. Thus the continental crust is normally much thicker under mountains, compared to lower lying areas.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Rock can [fold](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)") either symmetrically or asymmetrically. The upfolds are [anticlines](/wiki/Anticline "Anticline") and the downfolds are [synclines](/wiki/Syncline "Syncline"): in asymmetric folding there may also be recumbent and overturned folds. The [Balkan Mountains](/wiki/Balkan_Mountains "Balkan Mountains")[[26]](#cite_note-26) and the [Jura Mountains](/wiki/Jura_Mountains "Jura Mountains")[[27]](#cite_note-27) are examples of fold mountains.

### Block mountains

Main article: [Block mountains](/wiki/Block_mountains "Block mountains")

Block mountains are caused by [faults](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)") in the crust: a plane where rocks have moved past each other. When rocks on one side of a fault rise relative to the other, it can form a mountain.[[28]](#cite_note-Ryan-28) The uplifted blocks are block mountains or [horsts](/wiki/Horst_(geology) "Horst (geology)"). The intervening dropped blocks are termed [graben](/wiki/Graben "Graben"): these can be small or form extensive [rift valley](/wiki/Rift_valley "Rift valley") systems. This kind of landscape can be seen in [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) the [Vosges](/wiki/Vosges "Vosges") and [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine_graben "Rhine graben") valley,[[30]](#cite_note-30) and the [Basin and Range Province](/wiki/Basin_and_Range_Province "Basin and Range Province") of Western North America.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevin2010474–478-31) These areas often occur when the regional stress is extensional and the [crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") is thinned.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevin2010474–478-31)

### Erosion

Main article: [Erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Valle_Trebbia-%28scaled-and-sharpened%29.jpg/250px-Valle_Trebbia-%28scaled-and-sharpened%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valle_Trebbia-(scaled-and-sharpened).jpg)

[Apennine Mountains](/wiki/Apennine_Mountains "Apennine Mountains") and [Trebbia](/wiki/Trebbia "Trebbia") river, Italy

During and following uplift, mountains are subjected to the agents of [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion") (water, wind, ice, and gravity) which gradually wear the uplifted area down. Erosion causes the surface of mountains to be younger than the rocks that form the mountains themselves.[[32]](#cite_note-Fraknoi2004-32): 160 [Glacial processes](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") produce characteristic landforms, such as [pyramidal peaks](/wiki/Pyramidal_peak "Pyramidal peak"), knife-edge [arêtes](/wiki/Ar%C3%AAte "Arête"), and bowl-shaped [cirques](/wiki/Cirque "Cirque") that can contain lakes.[[33]](#cite_note-thornbury-1969-33) [Plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") mountains, such as the [Catskills](/wiki/Catskills "Catskills"), are formed from the erosion of an uplifted plateau.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Climate

Main article: [Alpine climate](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Ural_mountains_3_448122223_93fa978a6d_b.jpg/250px-Ural_mountains_3_448122223_93fa978a6d_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ural_mountains_3_448122223_93fa978a6d_b.jpg)

The [northern Urals](/wiki/Ural_Mountains "Ural Mountains") at high latitude and elevation have an alpine climate and barren ground.

Climate in the mountains becomes colder at high [elevations](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation"), due to an interaction between [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation") and convection. Sunlight in the [visible spectrum](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum") hits the ground and heats it. The ground then heats the air at the surface. If radiation were the only way to transfer heat from the ground to space, the [greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect") of gases in the atmosphere would keep the ground at roughly 333 K (60 °C; 140 °F), and the temperature would decay exponentially with height.[[35]](#cite_note-goodywilson-35)

However, when air is hot, it tends to expand, which lowers its density. Thus, hot air tends to rise and transfer heat upward. This is the process of [convection](/wiki/Convection "Convection"). Convection comes to equilibrium when a parcel of air at a given altitude has the same density as its surroundings. Air is a poor conductor of heat, so a parcel of air will rise and fall without exchanging heat. This is known as an [adiabatic process](/wiki/Adiabatic_process "Adiabatic process"), which has a characteristic pressure-temperature dependence. As the pressure gets lower, the temperature decreases. The rate of decrease of temperature with elevation is known as the [adiabatic lapse rate](/wiki/Adiabatic_lapse_rate "Adiabatic lapse rate"), which is approximately 9.8 °C per kilometre (or 5.4 °F (3.0 °C) per 1000 feet) of altitude.[[35]](#cite_note-goodywilson-35)

The presence of water in the atmosphere complicates the process of convection. Water vapor contains latent [heat of vaporization](/wiki/Heat_of_vaporization "Heat of vaporization"). As air rises and cools, it eventually becomes [saturated](/wiki/Dew_point "Dew point") and cannot hold its quantity of water vapor. The water vapor condenses to form clouds and releases heat, which changes the lapse rate from the [dry adiabatic lapse rate](/wiki/Dry_adiabatic_lapse_rate "Dry adiabatic lapse rate") to the [moist adiabatic lapse rate](/wiki/Moist_adiabatic_lapse_rate "Moist adiabatic lapse rate") (5.5 °C per kilometre or 3 °F (1.7 °C) per 1000 feet)[[36]](#cite_note-36)
The actual lapse rate can vary by altitude and by location.
Therefore, moving up 100 m (330 ft) on a mountain is roughly equivalent to moving 80 kilometres (45 miles or 0.75° of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude")) towards the nearest pole.[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19): 15 This relationship is only approximate, however, since local factors such as proximity to oceans (such as the Arctic Ocean) can drastically modify the climate.[[37]](#cite_note-37) As the altitude increases, the main form of [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation") becomes snow and the winds increase.[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19): 12

The effect of the climate on the ecology at an elevation can be largely captured through a combination of amount of precipitation, and the [biotemperature](/wiki/Biotemperature "Biotemperature"), as described by [Leslie Holdridge](/wiki/Leslie_Holdridge "Leslie Holdridge") in 1947.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Biotemperature is the mean temperature; all temperatures below 0 °C (32 °F) are considered to be 0 °C. When the temperature is below 0 °C, plants are dormant, so the exact temperature is unimportant. The peaks of mountains with permanent snow can have a biotemperature below 1.5 °C (34.7 °F).

### Climate change

Mountain environments are particularly sensitive to anthropogenic climate change and are currently undergoing alterations unprecedented in last 10,000 years.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The effect of global warming on mountain regions (relative to lowlands) is still an active area of study. Observational studies show that highlands are warming faster than nearby lowlands, but when compared globally, the effect disappears.[[40]](#cite_note-Pepin-40) Precipitation in highland areas is not increasing as quickly as in lowland areas.[[40]](#cite_note-Pepin-40) [Climate modeling](/wiki/Climate_modeling "Climate modeling") give mixed signals about whether a particular highland area will have increased or decreased precipitation.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Climate change has started to affect the physical and ecological systems of mountains. In recent decades mountain ice caps and glaciers have experienced accelerating ice loss.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The melting of the glaciers, permafrost and snow has caused underlying surfaces to become increasingly unstable. Landslip hazards have increased in both number and magnitude due to climate change.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Patterns of river discharge will also be significantly affected by climate change, which in turn will have significant impacts on communities that rely on water fed from alpine sources. Nearly half of mountain areas provide essential or supportive water resources for mainly urban populations,[[44]](#cite_note-44) in particular during the dry season and in semiarid areas such as in central Asia.

Alpine ecosystems can be particularly climatically sensitive. Many mid-latitude mountains act as cold climate refugia, with the ecosystems occupying small environmental niches. As well as the direct influence that the change in climate can have on an ecosystem, there is also the indirect one on the soils from changes in stability and soil development.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Ecology

Main article: [Montane ecology](/wiki/Montane_ecology "Montane ecology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/GlarusAlps.jpg/250px-GlarusAlps.jpg)](/wiki/File:GlarusAlps.jpg)

An alpine [mire](/wiki/Bog "Bog") in the [Swiss Alps](/wiki/Swiss_Alps "Swiss Alps")

The colder climate on mountains affects the plants and animals residing on mou
ntains. A particular set of plants and animals tend to be adapted to a relatively narrow range of climate. Thus, ecosystems tend to lie along elevation bands of roughly constant climate. This is called [altitudinal zonation](/wiki/Altitudinal_zonation "Altitudinal zonation").[[46]](#cite_note-46)
In regions with dry climates, the tendency of mountains to have higher precipitation as well as lower temperatures also provides for varying conditions, which enhances zonation.[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19)[[47]](#cite_note-Merriam-47)

Some plants and animals found in altitudinal zones tend to become isolated since the conditions above and below a particular zone will be inhospitable and thus constrain their movements or [dispersal](/wiki/Biological_dispersal "Biological dispersal"). These isolated ecological systems are known as [sky islands](/wiki/Sky_island "Sky island").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Altitudinal zones tend to follow a typical pattern. At the highest elevations, trees cannot grow, and whatever life may be present will be of the [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") type, resembling [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra").[[47]](#cite_note-Merriam-47) Just below the [tree line](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line"), one may find [subalpine](/wiki/Subalpine "Subalpine") forests of [needleleaf](/wiki/Pinophyta "Pinophyta") trees, which can withstand cold, dry conditions.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Below that, [montane forests](/wiki/Montane_forest "Montane forest") grow. In the temperate portions of the earth, those forests tend to be needleleaf trees, while in the tropics, they can be [broadleaf trees](/wiki/Broadleaf_tree "Broadleaf tree") growing in a [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest").

## Mountains and humans

See also: [List of highest cities in the world](/wiki/List_of_highest_cities_in_the_world "List of highest cities in the world")

The highest known permanently tolerable altitude is at 5,950 metres (19,520 ft).[[50]](#cite_note-highestHabitation-50) At very high altitudes, the decreasing atmospheric pressure means that less oxygen is available for breathing, and there is less protection against solar radiation ([UV](/wiki/UV "UV")).[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19) Above 8,000 metres (26,000 ft) elevation, there is not enough oxygen to support human life. This is sometimes referred to as the "[death zone](/wiki/Death_zone "Death zone")".[[51]](#cite_note-PBS-51) The summits of Mount Everest and [K2](/wiki/K2 "K2") are in the death zone.

### Mountain societies and economies

Mountains are generally less preferable for human habitation than lowlands, because of harsh weather and little level ground suitable for [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"). While 7% of the land area of Earth is above 2,500 metres (8,200 ft),[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19): 14 only 140 million people live above that altitude[[52]](#cite_note-52) and only 20–30 million people above 3,000 metres (9,800 ft) elevation.[[53]](#cite_note-53) About half of mountain dwellers live in the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes"), Central Asia, and Africa.[[20]](#cite_note-panos-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/La_Paz_Skyline.jpg/250px-La_Paz_Skyline.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Paz_Skyline.jpg)

The city of [La Paz](/wiki/La_Paz "La Paz") reaches up to 4,000 metres (13,000 ft) in elevation. Mount [Illimani](/wiki/Illimani "Illimani") dominates the city's skyline.

With limited access to infrastructure, only a handful of human communities exist above 4,000 metres (13,000 ft) of elevation. Many are small and have heavily specialized economies, often relying on industries such as agriculture, mining, and tourism.[[54]](#cite_note-54) An example of such a specialized town is [La Rinconada, Peru](/wiki/La_Rinconada,_Peru "La Rinconada, Peru"), a gold-mining town and the highest elevation human habitation at 5,100 metres (16,700 ft).[[55]](#cite_note-55) A counterexample is [El Alto](/wiki/El_Alto "El Alto"), Bolivia, at 4,150 metres (13,620 ft), which has a highly diverse service and manufacturing economy and a population of nearly 1 million.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Traditional mountain societies rely on agriculture, with higher risk of [crop failure](/wiki/Crop_failure "Crop failure") than at lower elevations. Minerals often occur in mountains, with [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") being an important component of the economics of some mountain-based societies. More recently, tourism has become more important to the economies of mountain communities, with developments focused around attractions such as [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park") and [ski resorts](/wiki/Ski_resorts "Ski resorts").[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19): 17 Approximately 80% of [mountain people](/wiki/Mountain_people "Mountain people") live below the poverty line.[[20]](#cite_note-panos-20)

Most of the world's rivers are fed from mountain sources, with snow acting as a storage mechanism for downstream users.[[19]](#cite_note-Blyth2002-19): 22 More than half of humanity depends on mountains for water.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In [geopolitics](/wiki/Geopolitics "Geopolitics"), mountains are often seen as [natural boundaries](/wiki/Natural_boundaries "Natural boundaries") between polities.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Mountaineering

This section is an excerpt from [Mountaineering](/wiki/Mountaineering "Mountaineering").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mountaineering&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/M_Rainier.jpg/250px-M_Rainier.jpg)](/wiki/File:M_Rainier.jpg)

Climbers ascending [Mount Rainier](/wiki/Mount_Rainier "Mount Rainier") looking at [Little Tahoma Peak](/wiki/Little_Tahoma_Peak "Little Tahoma Peak"), United States

[Mountaineering](/wiki/Mountaineering "Mountaineering") is a set of [outdoor activities](/wiki/Outdoor_activities "Outdoor activities") that involves ascending [mountains](/wiki/Mountains "Mountains").[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-Mountaineering_tour_crit-62) Mountaineering-related activities include traditional outdoor [climbing](/wiki/Climbing "Climbing"), [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing"), and traversing [via ferratas](/wiki/Via_ferrata "Via ferrata") that have become [sports](/wiki/Mountain_sports "Mountain sports") in their own right.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-Mountaineering_dx.doi.org-66) [Indoor climbing](/wiki/Indoor_climbing "Indoor climbing"), [sport climbing](/wiki/Sport_climbing "Sport climbing"), and [bouldering](/wiki/Bouldering "Bouldering") are also considered variants of mountaineering by some,[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[62]](#cite_note-Mountaineering_tour_crit-62) but are part of a wide group of [mountain sports](/wiki/Mountain_sport "Mountain sport"). In everyday language, the term "mountaineering" means mountain climbing or alpinism.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

Unlike most sports, mountaineering lacks widely applied formal rules, regulations, and governance; mountaineers adhere to a large variety of techniques and philosophies (including [grading](/wiki/Grade_(climbing) "Grade (climbing)") and [guidebooks](/wiki/Climbing_guidebook "Climbing guidebook")) when climbing mountains.[[62]](#cite_note-Mountaineering_tour_crit-62)[[69]](#cite_note-Mountaineering_Kublak2014-69) Numerous local [alpine clubs](/wiki/Alpine_club "Alpine club") support mountaineers by hosting resources and social activities. A federation of alpine clubs, the [International Climbing and Mountaineering Federation](/wiki/International_Climbing_and_Mountaineering_Federation "International Climbing and Mountaineering Federation") (UIAA), is the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee")-recognized world organization for mountaineering and climbing.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The consequences of mountaineering on the natural environment can be seen in terms of individual components of the environment (land relief, soil, vegetation, fauna, and landscape) and the location/zone of mountaineering activity (hiking, trekking, or climbing zone).[[71]](#cite_note-71) However, it should be borne in mind that tourism in mountain areas, by shifting the sector from agriculture to services, has a positive balance of change - agriculture is more invasive.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Mountaineering impacts communities on economic, political, social, and cultural levels, often leading to changes in people's worldviews influenced by globalization, specifically foreign cultures and lifestyles.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In terms of both its impact on the natural environment and local communities, the significance of this impact depends on the location in the vertical ([altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude") above sea level) and horizontal (zone) dimensions, and is therefore an example of [environmental determinism](/wiki/Environmental_determinism "Environmental determinism").[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Mountains as sacred places

Main article: [Sacred mountains](/wiki/Sacred_mountains "Sacred mountains")

Mountains often play a significant role in religion. There are for example a number of sacred mountains within Greece such as [Mount Olympus](/wiki/Mount_Olympus "Mount Olympus") which was held to be the home of the gods.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In Japanese culture, the 3,776.24 m (12,389.2 ft) volcano of [Mount Fuji](/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji") is also held to be sacred with tens of thousands of Japanese ascending it each year.[[76]](#cite_note-76) [Mount Kailash](/wiki/Mount_Kailash "Mount Kailash"), in the [Tibet Autonomous Region](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region") of China, is considered to be sacred in four religions: Hinduism, [Bon](/wiki/Bon "Bon"), Buddhism, and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"). In Ireland, [pilgrimages](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage") are made up the 952 metres (3,123 ft) [Mount Brandon](/wiki/Mount_Brandon "Mount Brandon") by [Irish Catholics](/wiki/Irish_Catholic "Irish Catholic").[[77]](#cite_note-77) The [Himalayan](/wiki/Himalaya "Himalaya") peak of [Nanda Devi](/wiki/Nanda_Devi "Nanda Devi") is associated with the Hindu goddesses Nanda and Sunanda;[[78]](#cite_note-78) it has been off-limits to climbers since 1983. [Mount Ararat](/wiki/Mount_Ararat "Mount Ararat") is a sacred mountain, as it is believed to be the [biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") landing place of [Noah's Ark](/wiki/Noah%27s_Ark "Noah's Ark"). In Europe and especially in the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), [summit crosses](/wiki/Summit_cross "Summit cross") are often erected on the tops of prominent mountains.[[79]](#cite_note-Eppacher-79)

## Superlatives

Main article: [List of highest mountains](/wiki/List_of_highest_mountains "List of highest mountains")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Comparison_of_Earth_farthest_points.svg/250px-Comparison_of_Earth_farthest_points.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comparison_of_Earth_farthest_points.svg)

Everest is highest from sea level (green), Mauna Kea is highest from its base (orange), Cayambe is farthest from Earth's axis (pink) and Chimborazo is farthest from Earth's centre (blue)

Heights of mountains are typically measured [above sea level](/wiki/Above_sea_level "Above sea level"). Using this metric, [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest") is the highest mountain on Earth, at 8,848 metres (29,029 ft).[[80]](#cite_note-80) There are at least 100 mountains with heights of over 7,200 metres (23,622 ft) above sea level, all of which are located in central and southern Asia. The highest mountains above sea level are generally not the highest above the surrounding terrain. There is no precise definition of surrounding base, but [Denali](/wiki/Denali "Denali"),[[81]](#cite_note-helman-81) [Mount Kilimanjaro](/wiki/Mount_Kilimanjaro "Mount Kilimanjaro") and [Nanga Parbat](/wiki/Nanga_Parbat "Nanga Parbat") are possible candidates for the tallest mountain on land by this measure. The bases of mountain islands are below sea level, and given this consideration [Mauna Kea](/wiki/Mauna_Kea "Mauna Kea") (4,207 m (13,802 ft) above sea level) is the world's tallest mountain and volcano, rising about 10,203 m (33,474 ft) from the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") floor.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

The highest mountains are not generally the most voluminous. [Mauna Loa](/wiki/Mauna_Loa "Mauna Loa") (4,169 m or 13,678 ft) is the largest mountain on Earth in terms of base area (about 2,000 sq mi or 5,200 km2) and volume (about 18,000 mi3 or 75,000 km3).[[83]](#cite_note-83) [Mount Kilimanjaro](/wiki/Mount_Kilimanjaro "Mount Kilimanjaro") is the largest non-shield volcano in terms of both base area (245 sq mi or 635 km2) and volume (1,150 mi3 or 4,793 km3). [Mount Logan](/wiki/Mount_Logan "Mount Logan") is the largest non-volcanic mountain in base area (120 sq mi or 311 km2).

The highest mountains above sea level are also not those with peaks farthest from the centre of the Earth, because the [figure of the Earth](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth") is not spherical. Sea level closer to the equator is several miles farther from the centre of the Earth. The summit of [Chimborazo](/wiki/Chimborazo "Chimborazo"), Ecuador's tallest mountain, is usually considered to be the farthest point from the Earth's centre, although the southern summit of Peru's tallest mountain, [Huascarán](/wiki/Huascar%C3%A1n "Huascarán"), is another contender.[[5]](#cite_note-Krulwich2007-5) Both have elevations above sea level more than 2 kilometres (6,600 ft) less than that of Everest.
